text: The wide hiatus and presence of a hiatus hernia are visible. The surgeon adopts the left crura approach, beginning with the division of short gastric vessels and identifying the left crura.

The assistant elevates the greater omentum near the lower pole of the spleen, while the surgeon applies counter-traction on the stomach near the greater curvature and begins dividing the omentum adjacent to the stomach.

The surgeon and assistant adjust traction and counter-traction on the stomach and surrounding structures to maintain clear exposure of the operative field, taking care to avoid injury to the greater curvature and posterior fundus.
The surgeon reaches the upper pole of the spleen and grasps the posterior fundus. The assistant grasps the posterior stomach, retracting it caudally and medially.
The surgeon divides the posterior gastric vessels and liberally severs the attachments of the posterior fundus to the left crus, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon dissects the hernia sac away from the stomach and esophagus, dividing it to increase esophageal length. The phrenosophageal membrane is also divided during this process.
The surgeon maintains ventral traction on the stomach, exposing the retroperitoneal attachments of the fundus for further dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection to facilitate further dissection from the right side, dividing the sac attached to the esophagus.

The surgeon identifies a well-developed sac due to the patient's large hiatus hernia. A gauze piece is placed at the base of the left rose for easier identification from the right side. The assistant grasps the fundus and retracts it as directed, while the past flaccida is divided.
An incision is made at the base of the right crus. The esophagus is looped with a tape, avoiding the mediastinum and left pleura, as the dissection is mostly completed.
The surgeon continues the dissection of the hernia sac on the right side, carefully releasing it from the esophagus.

The surgeon performs mediastinal esophageal mobilization circumferentially to gain intra-abdominal length, using alternating traction on the tape placed around the esophagus to enhance exposure for dissection.

The surgeon assesses the adequacy of mediastinal esophageal mobilization by ensuring visibility of the left crus from the right side and the right crus from the left side. Once satisfactory mobilization is achieved, the intra-abdominal length of the esophagus is estimated.

The surgeon performs the shoe shine maneuver to assess the adequacy and tension of the wrap, ensuring the fundus remains stable without slipping from the right to the left side, indicating adequate posterior immobilization. Chloroplasty is performed using two zero ethyl bond sutures on a double arm needle, with the thread divided on both sides and maintained at a length of about twenty centimeters.

After completing the crural repair, the surgeon passes a gastric calibration tube across the gastroesophageal junction to simulate a food bolus, ensuring the wrap is not excessively tight. Caudal traction is applied on the tape around the GI junction, positioning the wrap around the lower esophagus above the junction, avoiding placement at the cardia to prevent dysphagia and potential wrap migration.
The surgeon demonstrates the correct orientation of the suture line of the wrap at eleven o'clock and the cooler closure at one o'clock, ensuring proper alignment of these structures.
